hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Asking for Directions -- Finding Your Way Around the City on Foot
lesson.menu_desc: From the hotel door to the museum, on foot: how to ask for and understand walking directions in New York -- cross at the crosswalk, head up Fifth Avenue, it's around the corner. New words for the street (intersection, crosswalk, landmark, sidewalk, pedestrian, traffic light) and the grammar that makes a route clear: prepositions of place and movement (across from, next to, past, along, toward) -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 13 -- Asking for Directions
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 13 -- Asking for Directions

listenings.text: Sure, I can help you get to the Museum of Modern Art. It's not far at all, about a ten-minute walk from your hotel. So, from the front door here, turn right and head north on Fifth Avenue. Keep walking straight, and you'll pass a big church on your right and a bookstore on your left. When you reach the intersection with 53rd Street, you'll see a traffic light and a crosswalk. Cross Fifth Avenue there, and turn left onto 53rd. The museum is about halfway down the block, on your right, across from a small park. You really can't miss it. There's always a line of people on the sidewalk out front. If you get lost, just look for the tall glass tower on the corner. The museum is one block east of there. Oh, and one tip: stay on the sidewalk and watch the crosswalks, because the traffic here moves fast and the drivers don't wait for pedestrians. Have a great visit.

Hi Lucimara, it's Emma. I'm so glad we're having dinner tonight. Let me give you directions to the restaurant, because it's a little hidden and the map can be confusing. Okay, so when you come out of the station at Bedford Avenue, walk toward the water. That's west. Go straight for two blocks along Bedford until you reach a bakery on the corner. The restaurant is right around the corner from the bakery, on a quiet side street. It's next to a flower shop, opposite a small bookstore. If you pass the big painting of a whale on the wall, you've gone one block too far, so just turn around. The whole walk is about five minutes, all on the sidewalk, with no big streets to cross. Text me when you're close and I'll wave from the door. See you at eight.

items.situation: You leave your hotel and you need to find the Museum of Modern Art. Stop a stranger on the sidewalk and ask for directions -- politely.
tips: Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to the Museum of Modern Art?
Start with 'Excuse me, could you tell me...' -- the polite, indirect opener you learned last week. It works for directions too.

items.situation: A tourist asks you where the nearest station is. You can see it on the other side of the street. Tell them where it is.
tips: It's right across from us -- just cross at the crosswalk and it's on the corner.
'across from' tells you WHERE it is (opposite); 'on the corner' is the point where two streets meet.

items.situation: You're not sure which way to walk. Ask a local which direction the park is.
tips: Excuse me, is the park this way, or should I head the other way?
'head' means go in a direction: head north, head toward the river, head up the avenue.

items.situation: Give someone walking directions to a restaurant, using two landmarks.
tips: Walk past the bakery, and the restaurant is around the corner, next to a flower shop.
'past' is movement -- you go by it; 'next to' is place -- where it is. One sentence, both jobs.

items.situation: You need to cross a busy avenue safely. Say what you'll do.
tips: I'll wait for the traffic light and cross at the crosswalk.
You cross AT the crosswalk, not 'in' the crosswalk. And you wait for the traffic light, the red-yellow-green signal.

items.situation: Someone asks if the museum is far. Reassure them and give the distance.
tips: It's not far at all -- about a ten-minute walk, two blocks up Fifth Avenue.
'up Fifth Avenue' means along it; in New York, 'up' is north and 'down' is south.
